title_en: Prophetic Knowledge and the Superiority of Revelation Over Philosophy
title_ar: المعرفة النبوية وأفضلية الوحي على الفلسفة
content_en:
Having established that the claimed conflicts between reason and revelation rest on faulty philosophical premises, Ibn Taymiyyah turns in this section to a positive case for the epistemic superiority of prophetic knowledge over philosophical speculation in matters concerning the divine.

Ibn Taymiyyah's argument is not anti-intellectual. He does not disparage the use of reason in understanding the natural world, in establishing logical proofs for the existence and unity of Allah, or in evaluating ethical questions. What he argues is that in matters pertaining to the unseen — the nature of Allah, the reality of the afterlife, the details of divine decrees — revelation is the only reliable source of knowledge, because these matters are by definition beyond the reach of unaided human reason.

The philosophers had claimed that through rational demonstration (burhan) they could arrive at knowledge of the divine essence and attributes. Ibn Taymiyyah subjects these claims to historical scrutiny and finds them wanting: the greatest philosophers disagreed fundamentally among themselves on virtually every question of divine nature. Plato and Aristotle disagreed; al-Farabi and Ibn Sina differed; even within the same school, major positions evolved. If rational demonstration in this area produced genuine certainty, there would not be this persistent and fundamental disagreement.

By contrast, Ibn Taymiyyah argues, the prophets spoke with certainty about matters that no philosopher could verify — the nature of Paradise and Hellfire, the angels, the day of resurrection, the divine attributes — and the consistency of prophetic teaching across the succession of prophets is itself evidence of a common divine source. The prophets did not arrive at their knowledge through speculation but through divine revelation, which is a mode of knowledge unavailable to the philosopher.

A key concept in this section is what Ibn Taymiyyah calls 'al-mizan ash-shar'i' — the scriptural scale. Just as the philosopher has his rational scales for evaluating arguments, the believer has the Quran and authentic Sunnah as the ultimate measure of truth in matters of religion. When a philosophical argument produces a conclusion that contradicts what these scales establish, it is the argument that must be re-examined — not the scales.

Ibn Taymiyyah is careful, however, to distinguish between the content of revelation and human scholarly interpretation of that revelation. He does not claim that every scholarly opinion derived from the Quran and Sunnah is infallible — scholars err in interpretation. What is infallible is the Quran itself and the mass-transmitted Sunnah. This is why the methodology must always be: evaluate the rational argument, evaluate the interpretation of the text, and determine whether the conflict is genuine or a product of error on either side.

The section concludes with Ibn Taymiyyah's famous observation that the true heirs of the prophets are the scholars who transmit and apply divine knowledge — not the philosophers who set up their speculative systems as alternatives. The scholar who masters the Quran and Sunnah and applies sound reason in their service is a more reliable guide in religious questions than the most sophisticated philosopher who is disconnected from revelation.
title_id: Pengetahuan Kenabian dan Keunggulan Wahyu atas Filsafat
content_id:
Setelah menetapkan bahwa dugaan-dugaan pertentangan antara akal dan wahyu bertumpu pada premis-premis filosofis yang cacat, Ibn Taymiyyah beralih dalam bagian ini ke argumen positif untuk keunggulan epistemik pengetahuan kenabian atas spekulasi filosofis dalam masalah-masalah yang berkaitan dengan ketuhanan.

Argumen Ibn Taymiyyah bukanlah anti-intelektual. Beliau tidak meremehkan penggunaan akal dalam memahami alam natural, dalam menegakkan dalil-dalil logis untuk keberadaan dan keesaan Allah, atau dalam mengevaluasi pertanyaan-pertanyaan etis. Yang beliau argumenkan adalah bahwa dalam masalah-masalah yang berkaitan dengan hal gaib — hakikat Allah, realitas akhirat, rincian-rincian takdir Ilahi — wahyu adalah satu-satunya sumber pengetahuan yang dapat diandalkan, karena masalah-masalah ini secara definisi berada di luar jangkauan akal manusia yang tidak dibantu.

Para filosof telah mengklaim bahwa melalui demonstrasi rasional (burhan) mereka dapat sampai pada pengetahuan tentang zat dan sifat-sifat Ilahi. Ibn Taymiyyah menundukkan klaim-klaim ini pada pengujian historis dan mendapatinya lemah: para filosof terbesar berbeda pendapat secara fundamental di antara diri mereka sendiri tentang hampir setiap pertanyaan tentang hakikat Ilahi. Plato dan Aristoteles berbeda pendapat; al-Farabi dan Ibn Sina berbeda; bahkan dalam mazhab yang sama, posisi-posisi utama berevolusi. Jika demonstrasi rasional dalam area ini menghasilkan kepastian yang tulus, tidak akan ada perbedaan pendapat yang terus-menerus dan fundamental ini.

Sebaliknya, Ibn Taymiyyah berargumen, para nabi berbicara dengan kepastian tentang masalah-masalah yang tidak dapat diverifikasi oleh filosof mana pun — hakikat surga dan neraka, para malaikat, hari kebangkitan, sifat-sifat Ilahi — dan konsistensi ajaran kenabian lintas suksesi para nabi itu sendiri merupakan bukti sumber Ilahi yang sama. Para nabi tidak sampai pada pengetahuan mereka melalui spekulasi melainkan melalui wahyu Ilahi, yang merupakan cara pengetahuan yang tidak tersedia bagi filosof.

Sebuah konsep kunci dalam bagian ini adalah apa yang Ibn Taymiyyah sebut 'al-mizan as-syar'i' — timbangan skriptural. Sebagaimana filosof memiliki timbangan rasionalnya untuk mengevaluasi argumen-argumen, seorang mukmin memiliki Al-Quran dan Sunnah yang shahih sebagai ukuran kebenaran tertinggi dalam masalah-masalah agama. Ketika argumen filosofis menghasilkan kesimpulan yang bertentangan dengan apa yang timbangan-timbangan ini tetapkan, argumenlah yang harus dikaji ulang — bukan timbangannya.

Ibn Taymiyyah berhati-hati, namun demikian, untuk membedakan antara kandungan wahyu dan interpretasi keilmuan manusia terhadap wahyu itu. Beliau tidak mengklaim bahwa setiap pendapat keilmuan yang berasal dari Al-Quran dan Sunnah adalah ma'shum — para ulama keliru dalam interpretasi. Yang ma'shum adalah Al-Quran itu sendiri dan Sunnah yang mutawatir. Inilah mengapa metodologinya selalu harus: evaluasi argumen rasional, evaluasi interpretasi teks, dan tentukan apakah pertentangan itu nyata atau merupakan produk kesalahan di salah satu sisi.

Bagian ini ditutup dengan pengamatan terkenal Ibn Taymiyyah bahwa pewaris sejati para nabi adalah para ulama yang mewariskan dan menerapkan pengetahuan Ilahi — bukan para filosof yang mendirikan sistem-sistem spekulatif mereka sebagai alternatif. Ulama yang menguasai Al-Quran dan Sunnah dan menerapkan akal yang sehat dalam pelayanannya adalah panduan yang lebih dapat diandalkan dalam pertanyaan-pertanyaan agama daripada filosof yang paling canggih sekalipun yang terputus dari wahyu.
content_ar: Having established that the claimed conflicts between العقل and الوحي rest on faulty philosophical premises, ابن تيمية turns في هذا الفصل to a positive case for the epistemic superiority of prophetic العلم over philosophical speculation in matters فيما يتعلق بـ the divine. ابن تيمية's argument is not anti-intellectual. He does not disparage the use of العقل in الفهم the natural الدنيا, in establishing logical proofs for the existence and unity of الله, or in evaluating ethical questions. What he argues is that in matters pertaining to the unseen — the nature of الله, the reality of the الآخرة, the details of divine decrees — الوحي is the only reliable source of العلم, لأن these matters are by definition beyond the reach of unaided human العقل. The philosophers had claimed that through rational demonstration (burhan) they could arrive at العلم of the divine essence and attributes. ابن تيمية subjects these claims to historical scrutiny and finds them wanting: the greatest philosophers disagreed بشكل أساسي among themselves on virtually every question of divine nature. Plato and Aristotle disagreed; al-Farabi and Ibn Sina differed; even within the same school, major positions evolved. إذا rational demonstration in this area produced genuine certainty, there would not be this persistent and الأساسي disagreement. By contrast, ابن تيمية argues, the الأنبياء spoke with certainty about matters that no philosopher could verify — the nature of الجنة and النار, the الملائكة, the day of البعث, the divine attributes — and the consistency of prophetic teaching across the succession of الأنبياء is itself evidence of a common divine source. The الأنبياء did not arrive at their العلم through speculation but through divine الوحي, which is a mode of العلم unavailable to the philosopher. A الرئيسي concept في هذا الفصل is what ابن تيمية calls 'al-الميزان ash-shar'i' — the scriptural scale. Just as the philosopher has his rational scales for evaluating arguments, the المؤمن has the القرآن الكريم and authentic السنة as the ultimate measure of الحق in matters of religion. عندما a philosophical argument produces a conclusion that contradicts what these scales establish, it is the argument that must be re-examined — not the scales. ابن تيمية is careful, غير أن, to distinguish between the content of الوحي and human العلمي interpretation of that الوحي. He does not claim that every العلمي opinion derived from the القرآن الكريم and السنة is infallible — العلماء err in interpretation. What is infallible is the القرآن الكريم itself and the mass-transmitted السنة. This is why the methodology must always be: evaluate the rational argument, evaluate the interpretation of the text, and determine whether the conflict is genuine or a product of error on either side. The الفصل concludes with ابن تيمية's famous observation that the true heirs of the الأنبياء are the العلماء who transmit and apply divine العلم — not the philosophers who set up their speculative systems as alternatives. The scholar who masters the القرآن الكريم and السنة and applies sound العقل in their service is a more reliable guide in religious questions than the most sophisticated philosopher who is disconnected from الوحي.
